title: 6 4 Systems of ODEs
body:
6.4 SYSTEMS OF DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS

A system of homogeneous linear ordinary differential equations can be represented as a matrix equation, y0 = Ay. The system is easily solved by finding the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of A.

If A is a 3 Г— 3 matrix with linearly independent eigenvectors v1 , v2 and v3 , and associated eigenvalues О»1 , О»2 and О»3 , then the general solution to y0 = Ay takes the form

y = c1 v1 eО»1 t + c2 v2 eО»2 t + c3 v3 eО»3 t

The system of inhomogeneous linear ordinary differential equations

y0 = Ay + b(t)

can be solved using the transformation y = P z where P is the usual matrix of eigenvectors of A. In that case, the system becomes

z0 = Dz + P вҲ’1 b(t)

which can be solved using standard methods for first order linear differential equations.

We saw in the last lecture that a quadratic form пЈ« пЈ¶T пЈ« пЈ¶ x x пЈӯ y пЈё AпЈӯ y пЈё = 1 z z can be dramatically simplified by analysing the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the symmetric matrix A. We begin this lecture by proving the proving the validity of this algorithm. We will then extend these ideas to the analysis of systems of linear differential equations.

Because A = AT is a symmetric matrix, we know that it has a full set of orthogonal eigenvectors. We also know that the matrix of unit eigenvectors Q is an orthogonal matrix so that QT = QвҲ’1 . This means that the diagonalisation of A is represented by пЈ« пЈ¶ О»1 0 0 QT AQ = D, where D = пЈӯ 0 О»2 0 пЈё 0 0 О»3 is the diagonal matrix of eigenvalues.

This suggests that the quadratic form might have a simpler expression in a new coordinate system defined by the eigenvectors of A. To get to this new coordinate system, we rotate our coordinates x, y, and z using the orthogonal matrix Q (a rotation in space), пЈ« пЈ¶ пЈ« пЈ¶ x X пЈӯ y пЈё = P пЈӯ Y пЈё. z Z

The quadratic form becomes

пЈ« пЈ« пЈ¶пЈ¶T пЈ« пЈ¶ X X пЈӯP пЈӯ Y пЈёпЈё AP пЈӯ Y пЈё = 1 Z Z implying that

пЈ« пЈ¶T пЈ« пЈ¶ X X пЈӯ Y пЈё P T AP пЈӯ Y пЈё = 1 Z Z and hence we have

пЈ« пЈ¶T пЈ« пЈ¶ пЈ« пЈ¶T пЈ« пЈ¶пЈ« пЈ¶ X X X О»1 0 0 X пЈӯ Y пЈё D пЈӯ Y пЈё = 1 вҶ’ пЈӯ Y пЈё пЈӯ 0 О»2 0 пЈё пЈӯ Y пЈё = 1 Z Z Z 0 0 О»3 Z

As promised in the last lecture this yields the simplified form (without mixed terms) with respect to the principal axes:

О»1 X 2 + О»2 Y 2 + О»3 Z 2 = 1

We can now show that this same technique can also be used to dramatically simplify systems of linear differential equations.

SYSTEMS OF DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS

Example 1 Solve the system of differential equations.

y10 = 2y1 + y2 y20 = вҲ’y1 + y3 y30 = y1 + y2 + y3

where y1 (0) = 6, y2 (0) = вҲ’5, and y3 (0) = 7.

We begin by noting that the system may be written in matrix form as

y10 пЈ« пЈ¶ пЈ« пЈ¶пЈ« пЈ¶ 2 1 0 y1 пЈӯ y20 пЈё = пЈӯ вҲ’1 0 1 пЈё пЈӯ y2 пЈё y30 1 1 1 y3 which is expressed as y0 = Ay. The usual eigenanalysis yields eigenvalues 0,1, and 2 with пЈ« пЈ¶ пЈ« пЈ¶ пЈ« пЈ¶ 1 вҲ’1 1 associated eigenvectors пЈӯ вҲ’2 , пЈё пЈӯ 1 пЈё , and пЈӯ 0 пЈё. 1 0 1 Amazingly, a knowledge of the eigenvalues and eigenvectors is enough to find the solution of the system of differential equations! In fact, we can immediately write down the the solution as пЈ« пЈ¶ пЈ« пЈ¶ пЈ« пЈ¶ 1 вҲ’1 1 0t y = c1 вҲ’2 e + c2 пЈӯ пЈё пЈӯ 1 пЈё 1t e + c3 0 пЈё e2t пЈӯ 1 0 1 where c1 , c2 and c3 are constants of integration that can be fixed by specifying initial conditions. Reading across the rows we have a general solution:

y1 = c1 вҲ’ c2 et + c3 e2t

y2 = вҲ’2c1 + c2 et

y3 = c1 + c3 e2t

LetвҖҷs prove that this all works:

Claim: If A is a 3 Г— 3 matrix with linearly independent eigenvectors v1 , v2 and v3 , and associated eigenvalues О»1 , О»2 and О»3 , then the general solution to y0 = Ay takes the form

y = c1 v1 eО»1 t + c2 v2 eО»2 t + c3 v3 eО»3 t

where c1 , c2 and c3 are arbitrary constants.

Proof:

Method 1: Assume a solution to y0 = Ay of the form y = veО»t where v is a vector and О» is a number.

Method 2: Make the substitution y = P z where P is the matrix of eigenvectors of A.

It is clear from the above that once we have the eigenvectors and eigenvalues of A the solution to the system y0 = Ay is just one step away!

The initial conditions are implemented at the last stage to evaluate the three arbitrary constants. Recall that

y1 = c1 вҲ’ c2 et + c3 e2t

y2 = вҲ’2c1 + c2 et

y3 = c1 + c3 e2t

and that y1 (0) = 6, y2 (0) = вҲ’5, and y3 (0) = 7.

So

So we have c1 = 3, c2 = 1, c3 = 4.

Hence the final solution is

y1 = 3 вҲ’ et + 4e2t

y2 = вҲ’6 + et

y3 = 3 + 4e2t

These three functions satisfy both the system of differential equations and the i.c.вҖҷs. Lets check that the last equation y30 = y1 + y2 + y3 is satisfied:

LHS = y30 =

RHS = y1 + y2 + y3 =

F y1 = 3 вҲ’ et + 4e2t , y2 = вҲ’6 + et , y3 = 3 + 4e2t F

We can also use this method to solve systems of inhomogeneous first order linear differential equations, that is, equations of the form, пЈ« пЈ¶ b1 (t) y0 = Ay + b(t), where b(t) = пЈӯ b2 (t) пЈё b3 (t)

are functions of t. In this case we make the substitution y = P z where P is the matrix of eigenvectors of A. Thus,

z0 = P вҲ’1 AP z + P вҲ’1 b(t) = Dz + d(t)

where D = P вҲ’1 AP is the diagonal matrix of eigenvalues of A and d(t) = P вҲ’1 b(t). For a 3 Г— 3 matrix, we have z10 = О»1 z1 + d1 (t) z20 = О»2 z2 + d2 (t) z30 = О»3 z3 + d3 (t) We can solve these equations using the standard methods we learned for first order linear differential equations.

You can now do Q 93 and 94
